### Post by alex-desktop79 on 2007-08-06
I tried editing the screen resolutions (adding 1280x1024 to get that resolution) while installing the nvidia driver.
It doesnt seem to want to go to that resolution.

Here's my xorg.conf
```
# /etc/X11/xorg.conf (xorg X Window System server configuration file)
#
# This file was generated by dexconf, the Debian X Configuration tool, using
# values from the debconf database.
#
# Edit this file with caution, and see the xorg.conf(5) manual page.
# (Type "man xorg.conf" at the shell prompt.)
#
# This file is automatically updated on xserver-xorg package upgrades *only*
# if it has not been modified since the last upgrade of the xserver-xorg
# package.
#
# If you have edited this file but would like it to be automatically updated
# again, run the following command:
#   sudo dpkg-reconfigure -phigh xserver-xorg

Section "Files"
	Fontpath	"/usr/share/fonts/X11/misc"
	Fontpath	"/usr/share/fonts/X11/cyrillic"
	Fontpath	"/usr/share/fonts/X11/100dpi/:unscaled"
	Fontpath	"/usr/share/fonts/X11/75dpi/:unscaled"

	Fontpath	"/usr/share/fonts/X11/100dpi"
	Fontpath	"/usr/share/fonts/X11/75dpi"

	Fontpath	"/var/lib/defoma/x-ttcidfont-conf.d/dirs/TrueType"
EndSection

Section "Module"
	Load		"i2c"
	Load		"bitmap"
	Load		"ddc"
	Load		"extmod"
	Load		"freetype"
	Load		"glx"
	Load		"int10"

	Identifier	"Generic Keyboard"

	Option		"CoreKeyboard"

	Option		"XkbLayout"	"us"
EndSection

	Driver		"mouse"
	Option		"CorePointer"
	Option		"Device"	"/dev/input/mice"
	Option		"Protocol"	"ImPS/2"

EndSection

Section "InputDevice"
	Driver		"wacom"
	Identifier	"stylus"
	Option		"Device"	"/dev/input/wacom"

	Option		"ForceDevice"	"ISDV4"# Tablet PC ONLY
EndSection

Section "InputDevice"
	Driver		"wacom"
	Identifier	"eraser"
	Option		"Device"	"/dev/input/wacom"
	Option		"Type"	"eraser"
	Option		"ForceDevice"	"ISDV4"# Tablet PC ONLY
EndSection

Section "InputDevice"
	Driver		"wacom"
	Identifier	"cursor"
	Option		"Device"	"/dev/input/wacom"
	Option		"Type"	"cursor"
	Option		"ForceDevice"	"ISDV4"# Tablet PC ONLY
EndSection

Section "Device"
	Identifier	"nVidia Corporation NV34 [GeForce FX 5200]"
	Driver		"nvidia"
	Busid		"PCI:1:0:0"
	Option		"AddARGBVisuals"	"True"
	Option		"AddARGBGLXVisuals"	"True"
	Option		"NoLogo"	"True"
EndSection

Section "Monitor"
	Identifier	"SyncMaster"
	Option		"DPMS"
EndSection

Section "Screen"
	Identifier	"Default Screen"
	Device		"nVidia Corporation NV34 [GeForce FX 5200]"
	Monitor		"SyncMaster"
	Defaultdepth	24
	SubSection "Display"
		Depth	1
		Modes		"1280x1028"	"1024x768"	"800x600"	"640x480"
	EndSubSection
	SubSection "Display"
		Depth	4
		Modes		"1280x1028"	"1024x768"	"800x600"	"640x480"
	EndSubSection
	SubSection "Display"
		Depth	8
		Modes		"1280x1028"	"1024x768"	"800x600"	"640x480"
	EndSubSection
	SubSection "Display"
		Depth	15
		Modes		"1280x1028"	"1024x768"	"800x600"	"640x480"
	EndSubSection

Section "ServerLayout"
	Identifier	"Default Layout"
  screen "Default Screen"
	Inputdevice	"Generic Keyboard"
	Inputdevice	"Configured Mouse"
	Inputdevice	"stylus"	"SendCoreEvents"
	Inputdevice	"cursor"	"SendCoreEvents"
	Inputdevice	"eraser"	"SendCoreEvents"
EndSection

Section "DRI"
	Mode	0666
EndSection
```

I think the problem is just that the part with the resolutions isn't well laid out, if someone could post their xorg.conf for referance, I could probably sort it out myself.

### Post by Rocket2DMn on 2007-08-06
Have you tried reconfiguring X?  This tends to solve like 95% of resolution problems since manually editing Xorg rarely works.
```
sudo dpkg-reconfigure xserver-xorg
```  Select the nvidia driver on the correct page and when you get to the resolutions page, use TAB to move around and SPACEBAR to select/enable your monitor's highest resolution and everything less.  Then you can restart X with CTRL+ALT+BACKSPACE.  Be sure to save any open files as they will be killed when X restarts.  You should now be able to select your optimal resolution from System->Preferences->Screen Resolution

---

ah, the trusty method does not work, nice.

### Post by Rocket2DMn on 2007-08-06
If you want to reinstall to get around other problems, that's your call, but I don't think it will help you with your resolution problem.  A lot of people (myself included) get stuck with bad resolution after installing and have to run that configuration to get the resolutions correct.
If you take your time with the configuration, it asks you about your mouse, keyboard, video, and maybe some other hardware questions.  I do suggest that you read through it rather than just hitting ENTER.  You should be able to accept most of the default options, but reading will help you understand what's going on making it less likely that problems will come up.
I know it's a lot to push through, but it is the most effective way to get your resolution to the right size.
Do you have any specific questions about what it's asking you?  Feel free to post them and I'll help you through it.

---

### Post by JParkus on 2007-08-06
i did the sudo dpkg-reconfigure -phigh xserver-xorg
that xconf tells you to. Just be careful it will reset your restricted drivers if you are using beryl

### Post by alex-desktop79 on 2007-08-06
Actually, it was human error.
I entered 1028 instead of 1024.

Sorry for the trouble, but you'd think someone taking a look at the xorg.conf above could have noticed ;), no offense.

And IMO, reinstalling is a lot less trouble then fixing a linux bug, not that it's no trouble (depending on the bug).

### Post by hardyn on 2007-08-06
"I dont know 70% of the information the command above this post asks me."
"this time I'm in a rush"

Im sorry you are having so much trouble, but you have to look at the above 2 statements and ask yourslef a question...  I see two flaws, and neither of them are.

"Unfortunately, they are both unreliable."


The Nvidia driver is mostly automatic, and i have been able to install the Nvidia driver without editing the xorg.conf file at all, and i am running 1680x1050.
there are many good guides on this forum for the installation of the nvidia driver; and using "sudo nvidia-settings" almost eliminates any need to edit xorg.conf.

i know that breaking is the best way to learn, but you should perhaps take the suggestions of the above posters, and try to fix the driver problem, its not that serious, and you will learn much more than reinstalling every time there is a problem.

i fully understand being frustrated, but starting a help request with profanity is a good way NOT to get help with a problem; and this is a forum you may not get a responce in minutes or even hours, but 9/10 you well get a good response.

good luck.

---

### Post by mikesignguy on 2007-08-06
reinstalling the os every time you have a problem is  wrong.

There is no need. One of the benefits of having a non-monolithic operating system ( unlike windows).  

I have encountered many problems and have never reinstalled.   Everything from printer problems, no  wireless internet to hd failures.  In your defence , it is difficult to find the solution to your problem.   With me the problem has to do with knowing the correct question to ask in order to mind the proper solution.
